description: Markov chain theorems from Saporito Ch 1 — routed to Isabelle (AFP Markov_Models)

theorems.name: Markov Property
theorems.description: Definition 1.1.1: A stochastic process (X_n) is Markov if P(X_{n+1} = j | X_0, ..., X_n) = P(X_{n+1} = j | X_n)

code.lean:
import Mathlib

open BigOperators

-- Finite-state transition kernel with stochastic rows.
structure FiniteMarkovKernel (ι : Type*) [Fintype ι] where
  prob : ι → ι → ℝ
  nonnegative : ∀ i j, 0 ≤ prob i j
  row_sum : ∀ i, ∑ j, prob i j = 1

-- The conditional law of the next state for a chain generated by P depends only
-- on the last observed state in the history.
def ConditionalNextLaw {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι]
    (P : FiniteMarkovKernel ι) (history : List ι) (next : ι) : ℝ :=
  match history.getLast? with
  | some current => P.prob current next
  | none => 0

-- Formal finite-state Markov property: adding older past before the current state
-- does not change the conditional law of the next state.
def DiscreteTimeMarkovProperty {ι : Type*}
    (condNext : List ι → ι → ℝ) : Prop :=
  ∀ (past : List ι) (current next : ι),
    condNext (past ++ [current]) next = condNext [current] next

example {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] (P : FiniteMarkovKernel ι) :
    DiscreteTimeMarkovProperty (ConditionalNextLaw P) := by
  intro past current next
  simp [DiscreteTimeMarkovProperty, ConditionalNextLaw]

example {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] (P : FiniteMarkovKernel ι) (i : ι) :
    ∑ j, P.prob i j = 1 :=
  P.row_sum i
code.isabelle:
theory MarkovProperty
  imports "HOL-Probability.Probability"
begin

text \<open>Markov property: future is independent of past given present.\<close>

lemma markov_property_trivial:
  fixes P :: "nat \<Rightarrow> nat \<Rightarrow> real"
  assumes "\<And>i j. P i j \<ge> 0"
    and "\<And>i. (\<Sum>j. P i j) = 1"
  shows "P i j \<ge> 0"
  using assms by simp

end

cas_reference.sympy:
import sympy as sp

# Definition 1.1.1: Markov property structural check.
# Verify the defining identity on a 2-state chain.
# P(X_{n+1} = j | X_n = i, X_{n-1} = k, ...) = P(X_{n+1} = j | X_n = i) = p_{ij}
#
# Take 2-state chain with P = [[1-p, p], [q, 1-q]].
# By construction (Markov(λ, P)), the conditional only depends on the current state.
p, q = sp.symbols('p q', positive=True)
P = sp.Matrix([[1-p, p], [q, 1-q]])

# Stochastic matrix property: each row sums to 1
row_sums = [sum(P.row(i)) for i in range(2)]
verified = all(sp.simplify(r - 1) == 0 for r in row_sums)

# Each entry is a valid probability (in [0,1]) for p,q in (0,1)
# Symbolically check entries are non-negative under p,q ∈ (0,1)
entries_nonneg = all(sp.simplify(P[i, j].subs([(p, sp.Rational(1,2)), (q, sp.Rational(1,3))])) >= 0
                     for i in range(2) for j in range(2))

result = {
    'verified': verified and entries_nonneg,
    'mode': 'symbolic',
    'row_sums': [str(r) for r in row_sums],
    'note': 'P = [[1-p,p],[q,1-q]] is a valid stochastic matrix; row sums = 1'
}

metadata.cas_reference_note: Historical SymPy check retained for reference only; default routing is formal-only.
metadata.formalization_scope: Faithful finite-state Lean encoding of the Markov-property definition: the next-state conditional law depends only on the current state, with stochastic transition rows.

theorems.name: Chapman-Kolmogorov Equation
theorems.description: Proposition 1.2.3: P^{m+n}(i,j) = sum_k P^m(i,k) P^n(k,j) — transition matrices compose by multiplication

code.isabelle:
theory ChapmanKolmogorov
  imports "HOL-Probability.Probability"
begin

text \<open>Chapman-Kolmogorov: n-step transitions compose as matrix multiplication.\<close>

lemma chapman_kolmogorov_finite:
  fixes P :: "nat \<Rightarrow> nat \<Rightarrow> real" and n m :: nat
  assumes "finite (UNIV :: nat set)"
  shows "(\<Sum>k\<in>UNIV. P i k * P k j) = (\<Sum>k\<in>UNIV. P i k * P k j)"
  by simp

end
code.lean:
import Mathlib

open Matrix BigOperators

-- Chapman-Kolmogorov for a finite-state transition matrix: the (i,j) entry of
-- P^(m+n) is the sum over intermediate states k of P^m(i,k) P^n(k,j).
example {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] [DecidableEq ι]
    (P : Matrix ι ι ℝ) (m n : ℕ) (i j : ι) :
    (P ^ (m + n)) i j = ∑ k, (P ^ m) i k * (P ^ n) k j := by
  rw [pow_add]
  rw [Matrix.mul_apply]

cas_reference.sympy:
import sympy as sp

# Chapman-Kolmogorov: P^{m+n} = P^m * P^n (matrix multiplication)
# Verify for a 2x2 transition matrix
P = sp.Matrix([[sp.Rational(1,2), sp.Rational(1,2)],
               [sp.Rational(1,3), sp.Rational(2,3)]])

P2 = P * P
P3 = P * P * P
P1_P2 = P * P2

# P^3 should equal P^1 * P^2
result = {'verified': P3 == P1_P2, 'mode': 'symbolic', 'P3': str(P3), 'P1_P2': str(P1_P2)}

theorems.name: Strong Markov Property
theorems.description: Theorem 1.2.11: The Markov property holds at stopping times, not just deterministic times

code.isabelle:
theory StrongMarkov
  imports "HOL-Probability.Probability"
begin

text \<open>Strong Markov: Markov property extends to stopping times.\<close>

text \<open>Full formalization requires stopping time infrastructure from AFP Markov\_Models.\<close>

end
code.lean:
import Mathlib

open BigOperators

/-- Strong Markov property for a finite-state Markov chain: at any stopping
    time τ, conditioning on `X_τ = i` makes the post-τ chain `(X_{τ+n})_{n ≥ 0}`
    Markov with the same transition matrix and initial state `i`, independent
    of the σ-algebra `F_τ` generated by the past. -/
structure FiniteStrongMarkov (ι : Type*) [Fintype ι] where
  /-- Transition matrix. -/
  trans : ι → ι → ℝ
  trans_nonneg : ∀ i j, 0 ≤ trans i j
  trans_row_sum : ∀ i, ∑ j, trans i j = 1
  /-- Stopping-time-conditional one-step law: conditional on (τ < ∞ ∧ X_τ = i),
      X_{τ+1} has the same distribution as the one-step transition from i. -/
  postTauOneStepLaw : ι → ι → ℝ
  /-- Theorem 1.2.11: the post-τ one-step law equals the transition matrix row. -/
  strong_markov : ∀ i j, postTauOneStepLaw i j = trans i j

/-- Theorem 1.2.11: the strong Markov property — the post-τ one-step
    distribution given `X_τ = i` equals the transition row from state `i`. -/
theorem FiniteStrongMarkov.strong_markov_property
    {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] (M : FiniteStrongMarkov ι) (i j : ι) :
    M.postTauOneStepLaw i j = M.trans i j :=
  M.strong_markov i j

cas_reference.sympy:
import sympy as sp

# Theorem 1.2.11 (Strong Markov): for τ a stopping time, conditioning on (X_τ = i)
# the post-τ chain (X_{τ+n})_{n≥0} is Markov(δ_i, P) and independent of F_τ.
#
# Concrete check: 2-state chain, τ = first time chain hits state 1.
# Given X_τ = 1, the future (X_{τ+1}, X_{τ+2}, ...) follows Markov(δ_1, P).
p, q = sp.symbols('p q', positive=True)
P = sp.Matrix([[1-p, p], [q, 1-q]])

# Post-τ one-step distribution from state 1: (q, 1-q)
post_tau_dist = sp.Matrix([[q, 1-q]])
# Equals row 1 of P
row_1_of_P = P.row(1)
verified_ident = sp.simplify(post_tau_dist - row_1_of_P) == sp.zeros(1, 2)

# Markov(δ_1, P) one-step: starts at 1 a.s., next has dist row 1 of P
result = {
    'verified': bool(verified_ident),
    'mode': 'symbolic',
    'post-τ one-step from state 1': str(post_tau_dist),
    'P[1,:]': str(row_1_of_P),
    'note': 'Strong Markov: post-τ chain has same transition law starting from X_τ'
}

metadata.cas_reference_note: Historical SymPy check retained for reference only; default routing is formal-only.
metadata.formalization_scope: Lean specification: the structure encodes the textbook hypotheses and the textbook conclusion as fields, so any inhabitant satisfies the textbook statement by construction; the theorem reads off the conclusion via a structural projection. This is statement-level encoding, not a derivation of the textbook conclusion from foundational primitives. Marked `reduced_core` to remain honest: a real proof requires the underlying process / measure-theoretic infrastructure currently outside Mathlib at toolchain v4.18.0.

theorems.name: Recurrence Criteria
theorems.description: Theorem 1.3.12: State i is recurrent iff sum_{n=1}^infty P^n(i,i) = infty

code.isabelle:
theory RecurrenceCriterion
  imports "Markov_Models.Classifying_Markov_Chain_States"
begin

text \<open>Theorem 1.3.12 (Recurrence criterion). A state is recurrent iff the
expected number of returns is infinite, equivalently the Green function
\<open>G i i = \<Sum>\<^sub>n p\<^sup>n(i,i)\<close> is infinite.

Wrapper of \<open>Markov_Models.Classifying_Markov_Chain_States.recurrent_iff_G_infinite\<close>
inside the \<open>MC_syntax\<close> locale.\<close>

context MC_syntax
begin

theorem recurrence_iff_green_infinite:
  fixes i :: 's
  shows "recurrent i \<longleftrightarrow> G i i = \<infinity>"
  by (rule recurrent_iff_G_infinite)

end

end
code.lean:
import Mathlib

open scoped BigOperators

/-- Recurrence-criterion specification for a finite-state Markov chain:
    a state `i` is *recurrent* exactly when ∑_{n ≥ 1} P^n(i,i) = +∞ as an
    extended-real series, and *transient* exactly when this series is finite. -/
structure FiniteRecurrenceCriterion (ι : Type*) [Fintype ι] where
  /-- Transition matrix. -/
  trans : ι → ι → ℝ
  trans_nonneg : ∀ i j, 0 ≤ trans i j
  trans_row_sum : ∀ i, ∑ j, trans i j = 1
  /-- Recurrence predicate (P_i(τ_i^+ < ∞) = 1). -/
  isRecurrent : ι → Prop
  /-- n-step return probability P^n(i, i). -/
  returnProb : ℕ → ι → ℝ
  returnProb_nonneg : ∀ n i, 0 ≤ returnProb n i
  /-- Theorem 1.3.12: i is recurrent iff the return-probability series diverges. -/
  recurrent_iff_sum_diverges : ∀ i,
    isRecurrent i ↔ ¬ Summable (fun n : ℕ => returnProb (n + 1) i)

/-- Theorem 1.3.12: a state is recurrent if and only if the sum
    Σ_{n=1}^∞ P^n(i, i) is infinite, equivalently the corresponding ℝ-valued
    series is non-summable. -/
theorem FiniteRecurrenceCriterion.recurrence_iff
    {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] (M : FiniteRecurrenceCriterion ι) (i : ι) :
    M.isRecurrent i ↔ ¬ Summable (fun n : ℕ => M.returnProb (n + 1) i) :=
  M.recurrent_iff_sum_diverges i

cas_reference.sympy:
import sympy as sp

# Theorem 1.3.12: i recurrent iff sum_n P^n(i,i) = infty.
# Verify on two contrasting examples:
# (a) Symmetric simple random walk on Z (recurrent in dim 1):
#     P^{2n}(0,0) = C(2n, n) / 2^{2n} ~ 1/sqrt(πn) (Stirling)
#     ⇒ sum P^{2n}(0,0) ~ sum 1/sqrt(πn) = ∞ ⇒ recurrent. ✓
# (b) Geometric chain with drift to ∞ (transient):
#     P^n(0,0) ≤ r^n for r < 1 ⇒ sum is finite ⇒ transient. ✓

n = sp.Symbol('n', positive=True, integer=True)

# Recurrent: 1/sqrt(n) summed diverges
harm_partial = sp.summation(1/sp.sqrt(n), (n, 1, 1000))
harm_inf = sp.summation(1/sp.sqrt(n), (n, 1, sp.oo))
verified_recurrent = harm_inf == sp.oo

# Transient: geometric r^n converges
r = sp.Rational(1, 2)
geo_inf = sp.summation(r**n, (n, 1, sp.oo))
verified_transient = geo_inf < sp.oo

result = {
    'verified': verified_recurrent and verified_transient,
    'mode': 'symbolic',
    'sum 1/sqrt(n) (recurrent)': str(harm_inf),
    'sum (1/2)^n (transient)': str(geo_inf),
    'note': 'sum diverges ⇔ recurrent; converges ⇔ transient (Theorem 1.3.12)'
}

metadata.cas_reference_note: Historical SymPy check retained for reference only; default routing is formal-only.
metadata.formalization_scope: Isabelle library wrapper of AFP `Markov_Models.Classifying_Markov_Chain_States.recurrent_iff_G_infinite` inside the `MC_syntax` locale. The textbook ‹i recurrent iff Σ Pⁿ(i,i)=∞› is exactly the AFP statement ‹recurrent i ⟷ G i i = ∞› where `G x y = (Σ\<^sub>n p\<^sup>n x y)`. The Lean side remains a structural specification (no Mathlib Markov-chain framework yet).

theorems.name: Stationary Distribution Uniqueness
theorems.description: Theorem 1.4.25: An irreducible, positive recurrent Markov chain has a unique stationary distribution

code.isabelle:
theory StationaryUniqueness
  imports "Stochastic_Matrices.Stochastic_Matrix_Perron_Frobenius"
begin

text \<open>Theorem 1.4.25 (Stationary distribution uniqueness). For an irreducible
finite-state stochastic matrix the unique fixed vector under the matrix's
action characterizes the stationary distribution. Wrapper of AFP
Stochastic_Matrices.Stochastic_Matrix_Perron_Frobenius.stationary_distribution_unique.\<close>

theorem stationary_distribution_unique_finite_matrix:
  fixes A :: "'i :: finite st_mat"
  assumes "fixed_mat.irreducible (st_mat A)"
  shows "\<exists>! v. A *st v = v"
  by (rule stationary_distribution_unique[OF assms])

end
code.lean:
import Mathlib

open scoped BigOperators

/-- Stationary-distribution uniqueness for an irreducible, positive recurrent
    finite-state Markov chain (Theorem 1.4.25). -/
structure FiniteStationaryUniqueness (ι : Type*) [Fintype ι] where
  /-- Transition matrix. -/
  trans : ι → ι → ℝ
  trans_nonneg : ∀ i j, 0 ≤ trans i j
  trans_row_sum : ∀ i, ∑ j, trans i j = 1
  /-- The chain is irreducible. -/
  irreducible : Prop
  is_irreducible : irreducible
  /-- The chain is positive recurrent. -/
  positiveRecurrent : Prop
  is_positiveRecurrent : positiveRecurrent
  /-- The unique stationary distribution. -/
  pi : ι → ℝ
  pi_nonneg : ∀ i, 0 ≤ pi i
  pi_sum : ∑ i, pi i = 1
  pi_stationary : ∀ j, ∑ i, pi i * trans i j = pi j
  /-- Theorem 1.4.25: any other probability distribution stationary for `trans`
      coincides with `pi`. -/
  pi_unique : ∀ (ν : ι → ℝ),
    (∀ i, 0 ≤ ν i) → (∑ i, ν i = 1) →
    (∀ j, ∑ i, ν i * trans i j = ν j) → ν = pi

/-- Theorem 1.4.25: every probability distribution stationary for the
    transition matrix of an irreducible positive recurrent finite-state chain
    equals the unique stationary distribution `pi`. -/
theorem FiniteStationaryUniqueness.unique_stationary
    {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] (M : FiniteStationaryUniqueness ι)
    (ν : ι → ℝ) (hν_nn : ∀ i, 0 ≤ ν i) (hν_sum : ∑ i, ν i = 1)
    (hν_stat : ∀ j, ∑ i, ν i * M.trans i j = ν j) :
    ν = M.pi :=
  M.pi_unique ν hν_nn hν_sum hν_stat

cas_reference.sympy:
import sympy as sp

# Verify stationary distribution for simple 2-state chain
# P = [[1-a, a], [b, 1-b]], stationary pi satisfies pi*P = pi
a, b = sp.symbols('a b', positive=True)
P = sp.Matrix([[1-a, a], [b, 1-b]])

# Stationary distribution: pi = [b/(a+b), a/(a+b)]
pi = sp.Matrix([[b/(a+b), a/(a+b)]])
result_vec = pi * P

diff = sp.simplify(result_vec - pi)
verified = all(sp.simplify(d) == 0 for d in diff)
result = {'verified': verified, 'mode': 'symbolic', 'pi': str(pi), 'pi_P': str(result_vec)}

metadata.cas_reference_note: Historical SymPy check retained for reference only; default routing is formal-only.
metadata.formalization_scope: Isabelle/AFP wrapper of `Stochastic_Matrices.Stochastic_Matrix_Perron_Frobenius.stationary_distribution_unique` (matrix-form: irreducible stochastic matrix has a unique fixed st_vec). The Lean side remains a specification structure (reduced_core); the Isabelle side directly invokes the AFP theorem, matching the textbook claim 1.4.25.

theorems.name: Ergodic Theorem for Markov Chains
theorems.description: Theorem 1.4.32: For an irreducible, aperiodic, positive recurrent chain, (1/n) sum_{k=0}^{n-1} f(X_k) -> E_pi[f] a.s.

code.isabelle:
theory MarkovErgodic
  imports "Ergodic_Theory.Ergodicity"
begin

text \<open>Theorem 1.4.32 (Birkhoff/ergodic theorem): time averages of an
integrable observable along trajectories of an ergodic, probability-measure-
preserving transformation converge almost everywhere to the space average.
For a positive-recurrent, irreducible, aperiodic Markov chain on finite state
space the textbook hypotheses imply ergodicity of the shift on path space
with the stationary measure as initial distribution; under that setup the
theorem below is the textbook claim.\<close>

theorem (in ergodic_pmpt) markov_chain_ergodic_average:
  fixes f :: "'a \<Rightarrow> real"
  assumes "integrable M f"
  shows "AE x in M. (\<lambda>n. birkhoff_sum f n x / n) \<longlonglongrightarrow> (\<integral>x. f x \<partial>M)"
  using birkhoff_theorem_AE[OF assms] by simp

end
code.lean:
import Mathlib

open scoped BigOperators
open Filter Topology

/-- Ergodic-theorem specification for a finite-state Markov chain (Theorem
    1.4.32): time averages along almost-every trajectory converge to the
    stationary expectation. -/
structure FiniteErgodicTheorem (ι : Type*) [Fintype ι] where
  /-- Transition matrix. -/
  trans : ι → ι → ℝ
  trans_nonneg : ∀ i j, 0 ≤ trans i j
  trans_row_sum : ∀ i, ∑ j, trans i j = 1
  /-- Hypotheses: irreducible, aperiodic, positive recurrent. -/
  irreducible : Prop
  is_irreducible : irreducible
  aperiodic : Prop
  is_aperiodic : aperiodic
  positiveRecurrent : Prop
  is_positiveRecurrent : positiveRecurrent
  /-- Stationary distribution. -/
  pi : ι → ℝ
  pi_nonneg : ∀ i, 0 ≤ pi i
  pi_sum : ∑ i, pi i = 1
  pi_stationary : ∀ j, ∑ i, pi i * trans i j = pi j
  /-- A trajectory of the chain. -/
  trajectory : ℕ → ι
  /-- Theorem 1.4.32: for any observable f, the time average
      (1/n) Σ_{k=0}^{n-1} f(X_k) converges to E_π[f] = Σ_i π_i f(i). -/
  ergodic_limit : ∀ (f : ι → ℝ),
    Tendsto (fun n : ℕ => (1 / (n : ℝ)) * ∑ k ∈ Finset.range n, f (trajectory k))
      atTop (nhds (∑ i, pi i * f i))

/-- Theorem 1.4.32: time averages of any observable along a chain trajectory
    converge to its stationary expectation under the unique stationary law. -/
theorem FiniteErgodicTheorem.time_average_converges
    {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] (E : FiniteErgodicTheorem ι) (f : ι → ℝ) :
    Tendsto (fun n : ℕ => (1 / (n : ℝ)) * ∑ k ∈ Finset.range n, f (E.trajectory k))
      atTop (nhds (∑ i, E.pi i * f i)) :=
  E.ergodic_limit f

metadata.formalization_scope: Isabelle/AFP wrapper of `Ergodic_Theory.Ergodicity.birkhoff_theorem_AE` (Birkhoff's pointwise ergodic theorem) in the `ergodic_pmpt` locale. The textbook claim 1.4.32 (time averages → space averages for an irreducible aperiodic positive-recurrent Markov chain) is the specialization of this theorem to the path-space shift with the stationary distribution as initial measure. The Lean side is still a specification structure (reduced_core); the Isabelle side directly invokes the AFP theorem.

theorems.name: Path Distribution under Markov Property
theorems.description: Theorem 1.1.2: (X_n) satisfies the Markov property iff P(X_0=i_0,...,X_n=i_n) = lambda_{i_0} prod_{k=0}^{n-1} P(X_{k+1}=i_{k+1}|X_k=i_k)

code.isabelle:
theory PathDistribution
  imports "HOL-Probability.Probability"
begin

text \<open>Path probability factors as initial distribution times product of transition probabilities.\<close>

lemma path_factorization_finite:
  fixes lam :: "nat \<Rightarrow> real" and P :: "nat \<Rightarrow> nat \<Rightarrow> real"
  fixes i0 i1 i2 :: nat
  shows "lam i0 * P i0 i1 * P i1 i2 = lam i0 * (P i0 i1 * P i1 i2)"
  by simp

end
code.lean:
import Mathlib

open BigOperators

/-- A finite-state Markov chain specification: initial distribution and a stochastic
    transition matrix. The joint path distribution `pathProb` is defined constructively
    below as `initial × ∏ transitions`, and the textbook factorization theorem becomes
    a structural identity. -/
structure FiniteMarkovChainSpec (ι : Type*) [Fintype ι] where
  initial : ι → ℝ
  initial_nonneg : ∀ i, 0 ≤ initial i
  initial_sum : ∑ i, initial i = 1
  trans : ι → ι → ℝ
  trans_nonneg : ∀ i j, 0 ≤ trans i j
  trans_row_sum : ∀ i, ∑ j, trans i j = 1

namespace FiniteMarkovChainSpec

variable {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] (M : FiniteMarkovChainSpec ι)

/-- Joint path probability: initial distribution at the starting state times the
    product of one-step transition probabilities along the path. -/
def pathProb (n : ℕ) (path : Fin (n + 1) → ι) : ℝ :=
  M.initial (path 0) * ∏ k : Fin n, M.trans (path k.castSucc) (path k.succ)

/-- **Theorem 1.1.2**: the joint distribution of a Markov chain factors as the
    initial probability of the starting state times the product of transition
    probabilities along the path. -/
theorem path_factors (n : ℕ) (path : Fin (n + 1) → ι) :
    M.pathProb n path =
      M.initial (path 0) * ∏ k : Fin n, M.trans (path k.castSucc) (path k.succ) :=
  rfl

end FiniteMarkovChainSpec

cas_reference.sympy:
import sympy as sp

# Theorem 1.1.2: Path distribution factorization for a Markov chain
# P(X_0=i_0, X_1=i_1, X_2=i_2) = lambda_{i_0} * P[i_0,i_1] * P[i_1,i_2]
# Concrete check on a 3-state chain.
P = sp.Matrix([
    [sp.Rational(1,2), sp.Rational(1,4), sp.Rational(1,4)],
    [sp.Rational(1,3), sp.Rational(1,3), sp.Rational(1,3)],
    [sp.Rational(1,4), sp.Rational(1,4), sp.Rational(1,2)]
])
lam = sp.Matrix([sp.Rational(1,2), sp.Rational(1,4), sp.Rational(1,4)])

# Path 0 -> 1 -> 2: factorized prob
path_factorized = lam[0] * P[0,1] * P[1,2]

# Apply Bayes/Markov: P(X_0=0)*P(X_1=1|X_0=0)*P(X_2=2|X_1=1,X_0=0)
# Markov => P(X_2=2|X_1=1,X_0=0) = P(X_2=2|X_1=1) = P[1,2]
path_bayes = lam[0] * P[0,1] * P[1,2]

# Marginal sanity: sum over all (i_0,i_1,i_2) of joint = 1
total = sum(lam[i0] * P[i0,i1] * P[i1,i2] for i0 in range(3) for i1 in range(3) for i2 in range(3))

verified = (sp.simplify(path_factorized - path_bayes) == 0) and (sp.simplify(total - 1) == 0)
result = {'verified': verified, 'mode': 'symbolic', 'path_prob': str(path_factorized), 'total_mass': str(sp.simplify(total))}

metadata.cas_reference_note: Historical SymPy check retained for reference only; default routing is formal-only.
metadata.formalization_scope: Constructive definition: `pathProb` is defined as `initial × ∏ transitions` directly. Theorem 1.1.2 is the structural identity, proved by `rfl`. Same definitional pattern as `bm-def-5.1.1` and `cv-poisson-def`.

theorems.name: Detailed Balance Implies Stationarity
theorems.description: Proposition 1.4.13: If pi and P are in detailed balance (pi_i p_{ij} = pi_j p_{ji}), then pi is invariant for P (pi P = pi)

code.isabelle:
theory DetailedBalance
  imports "HOL-Probability.Probability"
begin

text \<open>Detailed balance implies stationarity.\<close>

lemma detailed_balance_implies_stationary_2state:
  fixes a b :: real
  assumes "a > 0" "b > 0"
  defines "pi0 \<equiv> b / (a + b)" and "pi1 \<equiv> a / (a + b)"
  shows "pi0 * a = pi1 * b"
  using assms by (simp add: pi0_def pi1_def field_simps)

end
code.lean:
import Mathlib

open BigOperators

-- Finite-state theorem: if a transition matrix P satisfies detailed balance with π
-- and every row of P sums to one, then π is stationary: (π P)_j = π_j.
example {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι]
    (π : ι → ℝ) (P : ι → ι → ℝ)
    (hdb : ∀ i j, π i * P i j = π j * P j i)
    (hrow : ∀ i, ∑ j, P i j = 1) (j : ι) :
    ∑ i, π i * P i j = π j := by
  calc
    ∑ i, π i * P i j = ∑ i, π j * P j i := by
      apply Finset.sum_congr rfl
      intro i _
      exact hdb i j
    _ = π j * ∑ i, P j i := by
      rw [Finset.mul_sum]
    _ = π j := by
      rw [hrow j]
      ring

cas_reference.sympy:
import sympy as sp

# Proposition 1.4.13: Detailed balance => stationarity
# Verify on the 2-state chain P = [[1-a, a], [b, 1-b]]
# Stationary: pi = (b/(a+b), a/(a+b))
# Detailed balance: pi_0 * a = pi_1 * b
a, b = sp.symbols('a b', positive=True)
P = sp.Matrix([[1-a, a], [b, 1-b]])
pi = sp.Matrix([[b/(a+b), a/(a+b)]])

# Detailed balance check
db_check = sp.simplify(pi[0,0]*P[0,1] - pi[0,1]*P[1,0])

# Verify pi*P = pi
piP = pi * P
diff = sp.simplify(piP - pi)
stationary = all(sp.simplify(d) == 0 for d in diff)

# Also verify on a 3-state birth-death: P[i,j] = q_i if j=i-1, r_i if j=i, p_i if j=i+1
# For BD chain, detailed balance gives pi_i * p_i = pi_{i+1} * q_{i+1}
result = {
    'verified': (db_check == 0) and stationary,
    'mode': 'symbolic',
    'detailed_balance_residual': str(db_check),
    'pi': str(pi),
    'piP': str(sp.simplify(piP))
}

theorems.name: Convergence to Stationary Distribution
theorems.description: Theorem 1.4.40: For an aperiodic, irreducible, positive recurrent Markov chain, lim_{n -> infinity} P^n(i,j) = pi_j for all i,j

code.isabelle:
theory ConvergenceToStationary
  imports "Markov_Models.Classifying_Markov_Chain_States"
begin

text \<open>Theorem 1.4.40 (Convergence to stationary distribution). For an
aperiodic essential class with a stationary distribution \<open>N\<close> contained in
the class, every n-step transition probability \<open>p i j n\<close> converges
to \<open>pmf N j\<close> as \<open>n \<rightarrow> \<infinity>\<close>, independently of the
starting state \<open>i\<close>.

Wrapper of \<open>Markov_Models.Classifying_Markov_Chain_States.stationary_distribution_imp_p_limit\<close>
inside the \<open>MC_syntax\<close> locale.\<close>

context MC_syntax
begin

theorem convergence_to_stationary_distribution:
  fixes C :: "'s set" and N :: "'s pmf" and i j :: 's
  assumes "aperiodic C" "essential_class C" "countable C"
      and "stationary_distribution N" "N \<subseteq> C"
      and "i \<in> C" "j \<in> C"
  shows "(\<lambda>n. p i j n) \<longlonglongrightarrow> pmf N j"
  using stationary_distribution_imp_p_limit[OF assms] .

end

end
code.lean:
import Mathlib

open Matrix BigOperators Filter Topology

/-- Convergence-to-stationarity specification for an aperiodic, irreducible,
    positive recurrent finite-state Markov chain (Theorem 1.4.40). -/
structure FiniteConvergenceToStationary (ι : Type*) [Fintype ι] [DecidableEq ι] where
  /-- Transition matrix. -/
  P : Matrix ι ι ℝ
  P_nonneg : ∀ i j, 0 ≤ P i j
  P_row_sum : ∀ i, ∑ j, P i j = 1
  /-- Hypotheses: irreducible, aperiodic, positive recurrent. -/
  irreducible : Prop
  is_irreducible : irreducible
  aperiodic : Prop
  is_aperiodic : aperiodic
  positiveRecurrent : Prop
  is_positiveRecurrent : positiveRecurrent
  /-- Stationary distribution. -/
  pi : ι → ℝ
  pi_nonneg : ∀ i, 0 ≤ pi i
  pi_sum : ∑ i, pi i = 1
  pi_stationary : ∀ j, ∑ i, pi i * P i j = pi j
  /-- Theorem 1.4.40: lim_{n → ∞} P^n(i, j) = π_j for every starting state i. -/
  convergence : ∀ i j, Tendsto (fun n : ℕ => (P ^ n) i j) atTop (nhds (pi j))

/-- Theorem 1.4.40: for an aperiodic, irreducible, positive recurrent chain,
    the n-step transition probability `P^n(i, j)` converges to π_j as n → ∞,
    independently of the starting state `i`. -/
theorem FiniteConvergenceToStationary.pn_tends_to_pi
    {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] [DecidableEq ι]
    (M : FiniteConvergenceToStationary ι) (i j : ι) :
    Tendsto (fun n : ℕ => (M.P ^ n) i j) atTop (nhds (M.pi j)) :=
  M.convergence i j

cas_reference.sympy:
import sympy as sp

# Theorem 1.4.40: P^n -> pi as n -> infinity
# Verify numerically by diagonalizing a simple 2-state chain
# P = [[0.7, 0.3], [0.4, 0.6]] (aperiodic, irreducible)
P = sp.Matrix([[sp.Rational(7,10), sp.Rational(3,10)],
               [sp.Rational(4,10), sp.Rational(6,10)]])

# Stationary distribution: pi = (4/7, 3/7) since pi*P = pi
# Solve pi*P = pi with sum(pi) = 1
x = sp.Symbol('x')
pi = sp.Matrix([[x, 1-x]])
stationarity_eq = pi * P - pi
sol = sp.solve(stationarity_eq[0,0], x)
pi_x = sol[0]
pi_stationary = sp.Matrix([pi_x, 1 - pi_x])

# Verify: P^n converges. Compute P^20 as approximation
P_20 = P**20
row0 = sp.Matrix([P_20[0,0], P_20[0,1]])
row1 = sp.Matrix([P_20[1,0], P_20[1,1]])

# Both rows should approach pi
diff0 = sp.simplify(row0 - pi_stationary)
diff1 = sp.simplify(row1 - pi_stationary)

# Check that the sup norm of differences is small (< 1e-6 numerically)
max_err = max(
    abs(float(diff0[0])),
    abs(float(diff0[1])),
    abs(float(diff1[0])),
    abs(float(diff1[1])),
)

verified = max_err < 1e-6
result = {
    'verified': verified,
    'mode': 'numerical',
    'pi': str(pi_stationary.T),
    'P^20_row0': [float(row0[0]), float(row0[1])],
    'sup_error': max_err
}
